title: Novel compaction approaches in the harness reference collection

questions.question: What compaction or context management strategies does each harness use? For each of the 11 harnesses (cersei, claurst, CodeWhale, codex, forgecode, goose, jcode, neo, pi, pi_agent_rust, yoagent), identify whether it implements conversation compaction, context window management, message pruning, or summarization. Describe the specific approach — not just that it exists, but how it works mechanically.

Which harnesses do something novel or different from simple truncation or summarization? Look for approaches like: sliding window with priority retention, semantic deduplication, tool result compression, hierarchical summarization, checkpoint/restore, context splitting across multiple calls, or anything else that goes beyond 'summarize old messages and prepend'. Describe each novel approach in detail.

How do the harnesses handle tool results during compaction? Tool outputs (file contents, command output, search results) are often the largest context consumers. Which harnesses compress, truncate, or replace tool results differently from regular conversation messages? What strategies do they use?

What triggers compaction in each harness? Is it token count, message count, a percentage of the context window, or something else? Do any harnesses compact proactively (before hitting limits) vs reactively (when the context is full)? What thresholds do they use?

context: The harness reference collection is at /Users/tom/Developer/tools/harness/ with 11 subdirectories, one per harness. Each is a different AI coding agent implementation. Look for compaction/context/summarization logic in their source code — typically in files related to conversation management, message handling, or context window management. Some are Rust, some TypeScript, some Python. Check for terms like compact, compress, summarize, truncate, prune, context, window, token in file names and content.
